intro_text:
Alang is not a beach anyone swims at. It is a 14-kilometer stretch of tidal mudflat on the Gulf of Khambhat, in Bhavnagar district, Gujarat, and it is the largest ship-breaking yard on Earth. More than a third of the world's retired cargo ships, tankers, and bulk carriers end their lives here, driven onto the mud and taken apart. The yard opened in 1983, when the MV Kota Tenjong was beached on 13 February, and it grew into a 14-kilometer line of some 183 cutting plots that scrap millions of tonnes of steel a year.

What makes Alang work is the tide. The Gulf of Khambhat funnels one of the highest tidal ranges in the world, around 13 meters, up its narrowing throat. At high tide a condemned ship is run aground at full speed, bow-first, as far up the mud as its momentum will carry it. As the tide falls, the ship is left high and dry, and the work begins: crews of laborers swarm the hull with cutting torches and cranes, stripping it from the top down, salvaging everything and reducing the rest to scrap that feeds India's steel mills.

The work is done by hand and it is dangerous. Alang employs on the order of 20,000 workers, most of them migrants from poorer states, Uttar Pradesh, Bihar, and Odisha, who live in shanties near the yards and return to their villages to farm during the monsoon. Between January 2009 and October 2012, local union figures recorded at least 54 workers killed in accidents. The hazards are falling steel, explosions in fuel tanks, and asbestos and heavy metals; the recycling pollutes the mud and water with heavy metals, and until Alang Hospital opened in March 2019 the nearest full-service hospital was 50 kilometers away in Bhavnagar.

This is where the most storied ships in the world come to die. The Seawise Giant, the longest ship ever built, was broken up here in December 2009. The Exxon Valdez, renamed Oriental Nicety, was scrapped at Alang in August 2012, twenty-three years after the Prince William Sound oil spill. And in 2006 the decommissioned French aircraft carrier Clemenceau, laden with asbestos, was turned back by India's Supreme Court before it could reach the yard, a case that put Alang at the center of a global argument about who is allowed to dump a toxic ship on whose coast.

That argument is slowly reshaping the place. After years of pressure under the Basel Convention, the Hong Kong International Convention for the Safe and Environmentally Sound Recycling of Ships entered into force on 26 June 2025, and 115 of Alang's 128 active plots now hold compliance certification, backed by Japanese-funded upgrades begun in 2017. And yet, by late 2025, Alang faced a deepening downturn, fewer ships, idle plots, a yard that observers began calling a dying industry. Alang is the recycling end of global shipping, kept cheap for forty years by a tide and by the men who work the mud, now being formalized and undercut at the same time.
honest_reckoning_note:
The honest reckoning at Alang is that it is the place where the global shipping industry externalizes the cost of its own death. Every cargo ship eventually becomes 20,000 tonnes of steel laced with asbestos, heavy metals, residual oil, and PCBs, and dismantling it safely is expensive. Beaching it on a tidal flat in Gujarat and cutting it apart by hand is cheap, and for decades the difference between those two prices was paid by migrant laborers in injuries, asbestosis, and death, and by the Gulf of Khambhat in heavy-metal contamination. The Clemenceau case in 2006 made the moral arithmetic explicit: a wealthy nation tried to send a toxic hull to a poorer coast, and was stopped only by a court.

The defenders have a real case too, and it should be stated fairly. Alang recycles ships rather than sinking or abandoning them; the salvaged steel is genuinely reused, and the yard sustains tens of thousands of livelihoods in a region with few alternatives. Since 2017, Japanese-funded upgrades and Hong Kong Convention certification have measurably improved many plots, with impermeable floors, cranes, and protective equipment that did not exist a decade ago. Critics such as the NGO Shipbreaking Platform argue the beaching method is inherently unsafe and polluting no matter how it is certified, and that certification risks laundering a practice that should be moved off the tidal flats entirely. Both things are true at once: Alang is safer than it was, and it is still a place where men cut apart poisoned ships by hand on a mudflat because it is the cheapest place on Earth to do it.
reckoning_pullquote: The world's ships come here to die cheaply, and for forty years it was the workers on the mud who paid the difference.

day_in_time.dawn: The day is set by the tide, not the sun. On a morning high tide a ship under tow is lined up offshore and then run at the mud under its own power, grounding with a long grinding boom that carries across the flats. By the time the water pulls back, the new hull is sitting high on the mud and the crews are already climbing it.
day_in_time.midday: By midday the full line of the yard is working: cutting torches throwing sparks down the flanks of half-dismantled hulls, cranes swinging steel plate to the ground, gangs hauling cut sections toward the re-rolling mills. The heat off the mud and the steel is brutal, and the bows of the largest ships stand like buildings over men who look tiny against them.
day_in_time.golden: Late afternoon brings the shift change and the salvage trade. Along the Bhavnagar road, the second-hand markets sell what came off the ships, furniture, lifeboats, generators, crockery, coils of rope, the entire fitted contents of a vessel laid out for resale. The low light turns the rust on the hulls a deeper red.
day_in_time.night: After dark the cutting mostly stops and the yards go quiet under watchmen and floodlights. The migrant workers return to the shanty settlements behind the plots, the rooms they share through the dry season before the monsoon sends them home to farm. The grounded ships sit black against the Gulf, waiting for the next tide and the next day's cutting.

food_drink.name: Workers' dhabas and canteens
food_drink.description: The food at Alang is the food of its migrant workforce: cheap, filling North Indian fare, dal, rice, roti, sabzi, served at roadside dhabas and plot canteens for the laborers from Uttar Pradesh, Bihar, and Odisha. This is functional eating for a working population, not dining for visitors.
food_drink.where: Roadside dhabas along the Alang–Bhavnagar road and within the yard settlements

food_drink.name: Talaja and Bhavnagar eateries
food_drink.description: The nearest towns, Talaja about 18 km away and the city of Bhavnagar about 50 km away, hold the proper restaurants of the region, serving Gujarati thali and Kathiyawadi food. Anyone staying overnight near Alang bases themselves in Bhavnagar.

food_drink.name: No visitor dining at the yard
food_drink.description: Honestly, Alang has no cafes, no beachfront, nothing built for an outsider. It is a restricted industrial zone. The only food on the flats is what feeds the men who work them.

timeline.title: Alang opens; the first ship is beached
timeline.description: The Alang ship-breaking yard is established in 1983, founded by N. Sundaresan, and the MV Kota Tenjong is beached on 13 February 1983 as the first vessel broken there. The yard will grow into a 14-kilometer line of cutting plots.

timeline.title: India turns back the Clemenceau
timeline.description: The decommissioned French aircraft carrier Clemenceau, carrying asbestos, is sent toward Alang in late 2005, but India's Supreme Court temporarily bars its entry in January 2006 amid protests over toxic-waste dumping. The ship is eventually scrapped in Britain. The case becomes a global flashpoint over exporting hazardous ships to developing coasts.

timeline.title: The Seawise Giant is broken up
timeline.description: The Seawise Giant, the longest ship ever built, is dismantled at Alang in December 2009, ending the life of the largest vessel in maritime history on the Gujarat mud.

timeline.title: The Exxon Valdez is scrapped
timeline.description: The tanker once named Exxon Valdez, by then renamed Oriental Nicety, is beached and scrapped at Alang in August 2012, twenty-three years after its 1989 Prince William Sound oil spill.

timeline.title: At least 54 worker deaths recorded (2009–2012)
timeline.description: Between January 2009 and October 2012, local union figures record at least 54 workers killed in accidents at Alang, a toll that crystallizes the yard's reputation as one of the most dangerous workplaces in the world.
timeline.source: Wikipedia: Alang Ship Breaking Yard (union data)

timeline.title: Alang Hospital opens
timeline.description: A dedicated hospital opens at Alang in March 2019. Until then, the nearest full-service hospital for the yard's tens of thousands of workers was 50 kilometers away in Bhavnagar.

timeline.title: The Hong Kong Convention enters into force
timeline.description: On 26 June 2025 the Hong Kong International Convention for the Safe and Environmentally Sound Recycling of Ships enters into force. By then 115 of Alang's 128 active plots hold compliance certification, the result of Japanese-funded upgrades begun in 2017.

timeline.title: A yard in decline
timeline.description: By late 2025 Alang faces a deepening downturn, with fewer ships arriving and many plots idle, prompting reports describing the world's largest ship graveyard as a dying industry even as it finishes certifying itself.

zones.name: The beaching grounds
zones.character: The tidal mudflat itself, where ships are run aground at high tide and left sitting in the open as the ~13-meter tide recedes. The defining surface of Alang: not sand for walking but the working floor of the largest ship-breaking operation on Earth.
zones.best_for: Understanding the beaching method that makes the whole yard possible
zones.notes: The Gulf of Khambhat's funnel produces one of the world's highest tidal ranges; the tide is the reason Alang exists where it does.

zones.name: The cutting plots
zones.character: The line of roughly 183 leased plots running along the 14-kilometer shore, each a separate ship-breaking operation with its own crews, cranes, and winches. This is where the hulls are stripped down to scrap, plot by plot.
zones.best_for: Seeing the scale of the operation; the half-dismantled hulls
zones.notes: 115 of 128 active plots are now Hong Kong Convention certified; access requires Gujarat Maritime Board permission.

zones.name: The salvage markets
zones.character: The roadside second-hand markets along the Bhavnagar–Alang road, where the fitted contents of the scrapped ships are resold: furniture, lifeboats, generators, electronics, crockery, rope, paint, the entire interior life of a vessel laid out for buyers.
zones.best_for: The publicly accessible face of Alang; seeing where a ship's contents go
zones.notes: The markets are open to the public, unlike the cutting plots themselves.

zones.name: The workers' settlements
zones.character: The shanty settlements behind the yards where the migrant workforce lives through the dry season, mostly men from Uttar Pradesh, Bihar, and Odisha who return to their villages to farm during the monsoon.
zones.notes: Housing, sanitation, and safety conditions for the workforce are a long-running subject of criticism and slow reform.

landmarks.name: The Alang–Sosiya beaching grounds
landmarks.description: The 14-kilometer line of tidal mudflat and cutting plots that constitutes the world's largest ship-breaking yard, extending into the adjacent Sosiya yard. The defining feature of the entire coast.

landmarks.name: The Alang salvage markets
landmarks.description: The roadside markets selling the salvaged contents of scrapped ships, a well-known fixture of the Bhavnagar–Alang road and the publicly visible end of the recycling chain.

landmarks.name: Alang Hospital
landmarks.description: The dedicated hospital that opened at Alang in March 2019, addressing the long-standing problem that the nearest full-service hospital for the yard's workers was 50 kilometers away in Bhavnagar.

cultural_refs.title: The Seawise Giant
cultural_refs.description: The longest ship ever built, broken up at Alang in December 2009. Its dismantling here made the yard the final resting place of the largest vessel in maritime history.

cultural_refs.title: The Exxon Valdez (Oriental Nicety)
cultural_refs.description: The tanker behind the 1989 Prince William Sound oil spill, scrapped at Alang in 2012 under the name Oriental Nicety, ending one of the most infamous hulls in environmental history on the Gujarat mud.

cultural_refs.title: The Clemenceau affair
cultural_refs.description: The 2006 standoff over the asbestos-laden French carrier Clemenceau, turned back by India's Supreme Court, which made Alang a symbol in the global debate over exporting toxic ships to developing-world coasts.

cultural_refs.title: Alang in shipbreaking journalism and advocacy
cultural_refs.description: Alang is a recurring subject of international photojournalism and of advocacy by groups such as the NGO Shipbreaking Platform, which campaigns against the beaching method on safety and environmental grounds.

recurring_events.name: The monsoon slowdown
recurring_events.when_text: Monsoon season, roughly June to September
recurring_events.description: Each monsoon, beaching and cutting slow sharply, and much of the migrant workforce returns to home villages in Uttar Pradesh, Bihar, and Odisha to work the agricultural season before coming back to the yards.

things_to_know.label: It is not a tourist beach
things_to_know.note: Alang is an active, restricted industrial zone, not a recreational beach. There is no swimming, no leisure, and no casual access to the cutting plots; entry to the yard requires permission from the Gujarat Maritime Board.

things_to_know.label: It is genuinely hazardous
things_to_know.note: The yard is one of the most dangerous workplaces in the world: cutting torches, falling steel, tank explosions, asbestos, and heavy-metal contamination. Any authorized visit is observation-only, with safety protocols.

things_to_know.label: Getting there is remote
things_to_know.note: The nearest airport is Bhavnagar (BHU), about 38 km away; the nearest town is Talaja, about 18 km; Bhavnagar city is about 50 km. The yard sits along the Bhavnagar–Alang coastal road.

things_to_know.label: The markets are the public face
things_to_know.note: The roadside second-hand markets selling salvaged ship goods are open to the public and are the realistic thing an outsider can actually see, unlike the plots themselves.

access_note: Alang lies along the Bhavnagar–Alang coastal road in Bhavnagar district, Gujarat. The nearest airport is Bhavnagar (BHU), about 38 km away; the nearest town is Talaja, about 18 km, and the city of Bhavnagar about 50 km, where visitors base themselves. The ship-breaking plots are a restricted industrial zone requiring Gujarat Maritime Board permission to enter; the roadside salvage markets are publicly accessible.
spike_explainer:
Alang is the place where the world's ships go to die, and the reason they die here rather than anywhere else is a tide. The Gulf of Khambhat narrows like a funnel on the west coast of India, and the water driven up it produces one of the highest tidal ranges on Earth, around 13 meters. That single fact of geography is what built the largest ship-breaking yard in the world on an otherwise unremarkable stretch of Gujarat mudflat.

**How a ship dies here.** When a cargo ship, tanker, or bulk carrier reaches the end of its working life, it is sold to a recycler and sailed to Alang under its own power for the last time. At high tide it is lined up offshore and then run straight at the beach at full speed, driving its bow as far up the mud as momentum will carry it. Then the tide goes out, and the enormous tidal range leaves the ship sitting high and dry on the flat, accessible on foot. Crews climb it with oxy-acetylene torches and cut it apart from the top down, dropping steel plate to the ground where cranes and gangs drag it off to be re-rolled into rebar for India's construction industry. Almost nothing is wasted: the steel, the machinery, the furniture, the fittings, all of it is salvaged and resold. The yard is a 14-kilometer line of roughly 183 separate cutting plots, and it dismantles more than a third of the world's retired oceangoing ships.

**Who does the work.** The dismantling is done overwhelmingly by hand, by a workforce on the order of 20,000, most of them migrants from poorer Indian states, Uttar Pradesh, Bihar, and Odisha. They live in shanty settlements behind the plots and many return to their villages to farm during the monsoon, when the yard slows. The work is brutal and dangerous. Between January 2009 and October 2012, local union figures recorded at least 54 deaths in accidents at Alang, and that count captures only the acute toll, not the slower damage of asbestos exposure, which causes disease years later. The hazards are constant: steel plate falling from height, explosions when torches meet residual fuel or gas in a tank, and the asbestos and heavy metals built into the older ships. The recycling contaminates the mud and the water with heavy metals. For most of the yard's history the nearest full hospital was 50 kilometers away in Bhavnagar; a dedicated hospital did not open at Alang until March 2019.

**The famous dead.** Because Alang takes the largest ships, it has taken some of the most storied hulls in history. The Seawise Giant, the longest ship ever built, was broken up here in December 2009. The Exxon Valdez, by then renamed Oriental Nicety, was scrapped here in August 2012, more than two decades after it fouled Prince William Sound. And in 2006 the asbestos-laden French aircraft carrier Clemenceau was sent toward Alang and turned back by India's Supreme Court, a case that became the defining global argument about whether a rich country may export its most toxic ships to a poorer country's coast for cheap disposal.

**The slow formalization.** That argument has been reshaping Alang. Under pressure from the Basel Convention on hazardous waste and years of campaigning, the maritime industry negotiated the Hong Kong International Convention for the Safe and Environmentally Sound Recycling of Ships, which finally entered into force on 26 June 2025. By then 115 of Alang's 128 active plots held compliance certification, the result of Japanese-funded upgrades begun in 2017, impermeable floors, cranes, protective equipment, and waste-handling that did not exist a decade earlier. Critics, including the NGO Shipbreaking Platform, argue that the beaching method is inherently unsafe and polluting and that certification risks legitimizing it rather than ending it. The yard is measurably safer than it was, and it is still a tidal flat where men cut apart poisoned ships by hand.

**What it is, now.** And it may be fading. By late 2025, with fewer ships arriving and plots sitting idle, observers were describing Alang as a dying industry even as it finished certifying itself. Whatever happens to it, Alang stands as the clearest physical fact of global shipping: that every vessel eventually becomes a beach full of hazardous steel, and that for forty years the cheapest place on Earth to deal with that fact was a Gujarat mudflat, and the cost was carried by the tide and by the men who worked it.

key_facts.label: Location
key_facts.value: Gulf of Khambhat, Bhavnagar district, Gujarat, India

key_facts.label: Scale
key_facts.value: ~183 plots along 14 km; >1/3 of world's scrapped ships

key_facts.label: The tide
key_facts.value: ~13 m range (Gulf of Khambhat) — enables beaching
key_facts.source: IIMS; Wikipedia: Alang Ship Breaking Yard

key_facts.label: Regulation
key_facts.value: Hong Kong Convention in force 26 Jun 2025; 115/128 plots certified

margin_notes.text: The Gulf of Khambhat funnels the tide into one of the highest ranges on Earth, around 13 meters. That range is the entire reason for Alang: it lets a ship be run aground and then left high and dry for hand-dismantling as the water retreats.

Alang scraps more than a third of the world's retired oceangoing ships across some 183 plots. The recovered steel is re-rolled into rebar for India's construction industry; almost the entire ship, down to the furniture, is salvaged and resold.

The workforce of roughly 20,000 is overwhelmingly migrant labor from Uttar Pradesh, Bihar, and Odisha, living in settlements behind the plots and returning to farm during the monsoon, when the yard slows.

Local unions recorded at least 54 deaths between 2009 and 2012, from falling steel and tank explosions; asbestos exposure adds a slower toll. A dedicated hospital did not open until March 2019; before that the nearest was 50 km away.

Alang took the Seawise Giant (the longest ship ever built, 2009) and the Exxon Valdez (as Oriental Nicety, 2012). In 2006 India's Supreme Court turned back the asbestos-laden carrier Clemenceau before it could land.

The Hong Kong Convention entered into force on 26 June 2025; 115 of 128 active plots are certified. Critics like the NGO Shipbreaking Platform argue beaching is inherently polluting and that certification legitimizes it rather than ending it.
